text: I will be talking to you about robotic arm-assisted total hip arthroplasty in developmental hip dysplasia.
This is Dr. Matthew Heppenstall, and you can see my co-authors listed here.

Total hip arthroplasty is an effective procedure in patients with developmental hip dysplasia and secondary arthritis, but there are several challenges presented, including acetabular bone deficiency and deformity, femoral deformity, such as abnormal femoral version and coxavalga, and also limb-link discrepancy.

This results in complex decision-making, including selection of an anatomic versus a high hip center, the need for acetabular augments or bone graft, and femoral implant choice, such as a cementless primary implant versus a modular, conical, or cemented implant for version control.
Finally, the surgeon has to select whether to perform a shortening osteotomy to manage limb length and prevent nerve compromise.

Outcomes of manual total hip arthroplasty and developmental hip dysplasia are generally good, but revision rates of nearly twenty percent have been reported at ten-year follow-up, with aseptic loosening being the most common cause of re-operation.
Side-ac nerve palsies of four percent or greater have been reported in the literature, and obviously these are things we want to avoid.
So why would we consider using a robot to help us perform a hip replacement in a patient with DDH?
Well, three-D CT modeling can help with our preoperative planning. Image-based navigation can improve our orientation during surgery.
And robotic accuracy and precision can improve execution of our surgical plan.
This is a case of a forty-three-year-old female with progressive left hip pain and limp. She has a history of bilateral hip dysplasia with childhood osteotomies.

She underwent a robotic right total hip arthroplasty one year ago. Physical examination revealed a coxalgic gait, painful and restricted range of motion, and a five millimeter limb length discrepancy shorter on the left.
Planning for these cases is performed on a computer before the surgery starts.
The surgeon examines the acetabulum and selects an appropriately sized acetabular component to fit the cavity from front to back, ensuring not to ream through the anterior and posterior walls.

once the proper size has been selected we can look at its proximal distal position make sure we're going to have enough superior coverage to achieve fixation and this can be looked at in both the sagittal and coronal planes we can make sure we're medial enough to get the support that we need on the femur we can look and see whether we're going to be able to put our femoral component in the desired anteversion in this case i can see that the maximally retroverting the femoral stem within the bone, I still have more than twenty degrees of femoral anteversion, so I won't be able to use the tapered wedge implant that is on label with this system.
The surgeon selects a conical stem for the femoral component, similar to the one used on the contralateral side, to address the anatomical requirements of the left hip.
So this particular case was done through a posterior approach, as you can see here. These cases can also be done through a direct
lateral approach, an anterolateral approach, or direct anterior approach depending on the preference of the surgeon and the particulars of the case.
The surgeon places a checkpoint on the greater trochanter and inserts a ray anchored with three Stein pins into the iliac crest percutaneously.

The surgeon uses a stabilizer for acetabular orientation, ensuring it is fully seated on the iliac crest. The baseline limb length and offset are checked, followed by dislocating the hip to measure the lesser to center distance using a robotic system.
If we have a baseline information here, we can use that information to understand whether we've lengthened or shortened the leg without a trial reduction later in surgery.

The surgeon cuts the femoral neck and removes the femoral head, followed by soft tissue releases to improve acetabular exposure. Due to significant scarring from a previous pelvic osteotomy, the redundant acetabular labrum is removed, and a checkpoint is placed on the pelvis.

The surgeon registers the checkpoint and the three divots on the stabilizer for the acetabulum. Points are taken both inside the acetabular concavity and around the periphery to register the acetabular bone.
The surgeon confirms the registration by observing the popping of bubbles, indicating an accurate match with the native anatomy. The setup is now ready for the introduction of a robotic system.

The surgeon performs the initial robotic reaming of the acetabulum using a reamer that is two millimeters smaller than the final target size. Due to dysplasia, the reamer may skip if the acetabular concavity is not well-defined. The center of rotation is established during this process.

The surgeon uses a robotic arm to ream the acetabular bone to the final target size, achieving a concentric ream. A multi-hole component is then brought in and guided into position with the robotic arm, ensuring proper inclination and aversion. The component achieves an excellent press fit despite the acetabular bone deficiency.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to take five points around the margin of the acetabular component, visually assessing the alignment and version relative to the planned cut position and bony landmarks.
The surgeon uses multiple screws to supplement the fixation of the acetabular component. The focus then shifts to the femur for the next stage of the procedure.

The surgeon uses a box osteotome to access the lateral aspect of the femur. A conical implant is prepared by reaming the femoral diaphysis. The posterior femoral neck is partially removed using a Midas instrument to adjust the stem placement, achieving approximately fifteen degrees of anteversion.

The surgeon measures the lesser-to-center distance, noting an increase of a few millimeters due to the distal shift of the acetabular center. A trial reduction is performed, revealing the limb is approximately seven millimeters longer than the contralateral side. The surgeon evaluates a pseudo-intraoperative x-ray and determines the limb appears too long.

The surgeon removes the trial implant and reams deeper into the femur before inserting the actual device several millimeters deeper, along with the planned head. The hip is reduced, and checkpoints and arrays are removed. The hip capsule is closed using a transosseous repair, followed by closure of the quadratus femoris. A layered closure is performed from the fascia up to the skin.

This patient had no complications. By two weeks, she was walking well without assistive devices. She had painless hip range of motion and an apparent limb length difference of two millimeters longer on the left than the right.

Looking at our own published outcomes in these patients, we reported on twenty-six patients with mean two-year follow-up, six of whom had Cro-III or IV dysplasia. We were able to get away with using a tapered wedge implant in twenty of these patients. Four were treated with a cementless conical implant and two required cemented stems.

All had cementless cups with no need for augment or structural graft. Here's an example of a case, and you can see here the post-operative x-ray. No patients had a limb length discrepancy greater than five millimeters, except one patient who had bilateral hip disease, and that discrepancy equalized after the contralateral hip surgery. There were no malpositioned cups, no dislocations, no infections, and no complications attributed to the robot. There were no reoperations for acid tabular fixation failure.

There was one femoral revision for fracture and loosening, and mean Harris hip score improvement was thirty-one points. In conclusion, three-D planning is particularly useful in severe hip dysplasia. The robot facilitates precise execution of a complex surgical plan and helps us minimize technical complications. These are our references.
